questions.question: What is the Job Demand-Resource (JD-R) model and why does it matter for workplace stress?

options.text: A model that says all work stress is caused by personal weakness
A model showing that job strain (high demands + low control/resources) predicts chronic health problems—distinguishing structural workplace issues from individual coping capacity
A productivity framework unrelated to health
A model that only applies to blue-collar work

questions.explanation: The Job Demand-Resource model is a foundational framework in occupational health psychology. It shows that job strain—the combination of high job demands (workload, time pressure, emotional labor) with low job control/resources (autonomy, support, decision latitude)—is a significant independent risk factor for chronic health problems including cardiovascular disease, depression, and burnout. This model matters because it distinguishes modifiable individual factors (personal coping skills) from structural workplace factors (organizational design, staffing, leadership) that require systemic intervention. When demands genuinely exceed resources due to organizational issues, individual stress management cannot and should not be the sole solution.

questions.question: What is the evidence-based distinction between occupational stress (manageable with skills) and burnout (requires organizational intervention)?

options.text: They are the same thing

Occupational stress is situational and responsive to individual coping strategies; burnout is chronic exhaustion, cynicism, and reduced efficacy resulting from sustained job strain—individual interventions alone cannot prevent burnout
Only weak people experience burnout
Burnout is just a medical diagnosis unrelated to workplace conditions

questions.explanation: This distinction is critical for avoiding victim-blaming. Occupational stress is the experience of pressure, challenge, or demand at work—it fluctuates and can be managed with individual coping strategies when demands are reasonable and resources adequate. Burnout, by contrast, is a syndrome of chronic emotional exhaustion, cynicism/depersonalization, and reduced professional efficacy resulting from sustained job strain—typically chronic workload exceeding capacity, lack of control, insufficient recognition, or value conflicts. Research shows individual stress management can help with everyday work stress, but burnout prevention requires organizational intervention: workload management, adequate staffing, supportive leadership, fair treatment, and alignment between job demands and resources. The research brief explicitly warns: 'No self-guided program has evidence for permanent burnout prevention.'

questions.question: Why is chronic workplace abuse or harassment not addressable through individual stress management skills?

options.text: Individual resilience skills can handle all workplace situations

Because workplace abuse/harassment is a structural safety issue requiring HR/legal intervention—framing it as an individual stress management problem is victim-blaming and delays necessary protective action
Workplace abuse doesn't really exist
Only physical violence, not psychological abuse, requires intervention

questions.explanation: The research brief explicitly identifies chronic workplace abuse/harassment as a condition where 'individual resilience reframing is victim-blaming; structural intervention and legal/HR escalation required.' Workplace abuse (bullying, harassment, discrimination, hostile work environment) is not a stress management problem—it's a safety and legal issue. Attempting to address it through individual coping skills: (1) inappropriately places responsibility on the person being harmed, (2) implies they could prevent abuse through better stress management (which is false and harmful), (3) delays necessary protective intervention (HR, legal support, advocacy resources), and (4) allows harmful organizational conditions to persist. Stress management education must clearly distinguish everyday work stress (skill-responsive) from abusive conditions (requiring external intervention).

questions.question: What workplace conditions indicate that individual stress management is insufficient and organizational change is needed?

options.text: Any time you feel stressed at work

Chronic understaffing, consistently unrealistic deadlines, toxic leadership, harassment, unsafe conditions, or workload that exceeds capacity despite reasonable effort—these require organizational intervention
Individual stress management should handle all workplace issues
Only physical safety hazards require organizational intervention

questions.explanation: Several workplace conditions signal that the problem is structural, not individual: chronic understaffing (where workload exceeds what any reasonable person could handle), consistently unrealistic deadlines that cannot be met despite competent effort, toxic leadership (bullying, favoritism, unclear expectations, undermining), harassment or discrimination, psychologically unsafe work environment, or role demands that fundamentally exceed role resources. In these situations, individual stress management skills may help you cope temporarily, but they cannot and should not be expected to compensate for organizational dysfunction. Appropriate responses include: advocating for organizational change, HR consultation, union/labor resources, legal consultation for harassment/discrimination, or in severe cases, job transition for personal safety.

questions.question: When is workplace stress a medical or mental health issue requiring clinical referral rather than just stress management skills?

options.text: Never—all workplace stress can be self-managed
When work stress leads to severe burnout with depression, suicidal ideation, panic attacks, substance misuse as coping, or inability to maintain basic functioning at work or home
Only if you are hospitalized
Workplace stress never requires professional mental health support

questions.explanation: Workplace stress crosses into clinical territory requiring professional support when it results in: severe burnout accompanied by major depression (persistent hopelessness, anhedonia, sleep/appetite changes), active suicidal ideation or self-harm, panic attacks or severe anxiety impairing function, substance misuse as a coping mechanism, inability to maintain basic functioning (can't get to work, can't complete essential tasks, significant relationship damage), or physical health consequences (chest pain, severe headaches, gastrointestinal issues). These symptoms indicate that stress has contributed to or triggered clinical conditions requiring assessment and treatment from a mental health professional, not just workplace stress management skills. The individual may also need medical leave or workplace accommodations during treatment.
